findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 7

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 19 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to hear directly from the people who actually experience this pain — add a slide showing 3-4 customer conversations or pilot results that prove developers and villa owners are actively frustrated with current systems and would buy your solution (e.g., "Interviewed 12 villa developers in Dubai; 10 cited corrosion and maintenance as reasons they avoid solar hot water despite mandate" or "Pilot with [Developer Name] showed 40% reduction in warranty claims"). This transforms your market insight from logical to lived.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist rather than a timeline—add a specific regulatory deadline or enforcement date to show why developers need to solve this in the next 12-18 months, not eventually. For example, if Dubai's Estidama standards require all new villas to have functioning solar hot water by Q3 2025, or if the UAE Climate Law penalties kick in next year, lead with that date and connect it directly to your addressable market (the pipeline of villas under construction right now that will fail inspection without your solution).

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel); Solar hot water mandated by regulation; Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature roadmap, not a defensible technology. Add one concrete example showing how your AI actually works: for instance, "HelioHelp ingests building thermal data, solar irradiance patterns, and water demand profiles to auto-generate a system design in under 5 minutes—something competitors require 2-3 weeks of manual engineering for." This specificity proves you've solved a real technical problem, not just wrapped existing tools in an AI label.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're building a platform that depends on AI working reliably at scale, but you haven't mentioned what cloud infrastructure, APIs, or third-party services power HelioHelp — this matters to investors because it affects your unit economics, scalability limits, and risk profile. Add a brief technical dependencies section to your appendix that names your cloud provider (e.g., "HelioHelp runs on AWS with auto-scaling to handle 10,000+ concurrent design requests"), any critical third-party APIs you rely on (weather data, mapping services), and how you've mitigated lock-in risk — this transforms a vague "AI platform" into a credible, de-risked technical architecture.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi) with mandated solar installations and commercial deployments handling customer data through your AI platform — add a single slide in the Appendix addressing how you handle data security, certifications, and compliance (e.g., "ISO 27001 roadmap," "customer data encryption standards," "compliance with UAE solar installation regulations") so investors don't have to ask about it during diligence. This is especially important since HelioHelp collects building details and performance data from installers and homeowners globally.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides present financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a single "Assumptions" box to your financials slide that shows the three drivers behind your Base case: units sold per year (starting with your 50-unit target by end of 2026, then scaling to X units annually), average hardware revenue per unit (e.g., $15K based on your Rixos deal at $207K for 38 systems), and gross margin ramp (35% in 2026 climbing to 45% by 2027 as you hit local manufacturing targets). This transforms "conservative projections" from a claim into a transparent, testable story that investors can actually stress-test themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: $350K seed funding: 18-month cash runway

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see the full unit economics story—specifically CAC and LTV for your core customer segments (hotels vs. villas) so they can understand how quickly you recover customer acquisition costs. Add a simple table on Slide 14 showing: for hotels, what's your average deal size ($207K for Rixos?), estimated CAC (sales/marketing cost per hotel project), and payback period; same for villas using your EtihadWE pilot ($6K per unit) as a baseline—this transforms your margins into a credibility signal that you've thought through repeatability

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted figure on Slide 12 is your actual committed revenue, while the $0.3M on Slide 14 includes projected upside from the pipeline discussions below it—clarify this by labeling Slide 14's 2026 hardware revenue as "$213K contracted + $87K pipeline" so investors see you're not double-counting, just showing how the Rixos deal plus partial conversion of EtihadWE/Masdar discussions gets you to $300K.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy the new capital you're raising—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing tooling: $X," "Sales & ops hires: $X," "Pilot expansion: $X," "Working capital: $X") with a timeline showing when each dollar gets spent over the next 18 months. Right now investors see your $350K seed runway and your $213K pipeline, but they don't know what portion of new funding goes to scaling production versus hiring versus customer acquisition, which makes it hard for them to assess execution risk.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: EBITDA positive 2027

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment received or customer status
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under each customer name stating payment status — for example, "Rixos (live project, $X in revenue received Q3 2024)" and "EtihadWE (pilot phase, payment pending Q1 2025)". This tells investors immediately which relationships have converted to actual cash versus which are still in progress, removing any ambiguity about your paying customer count.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue movement—add specific numbers like "Revenue grew from $X in Month 1 to $Y in Month 3" or "Q1 2024 revenue: $180K, Q2 2024: $320K" to prove the business is accelerating, not just positioned to scale. Even if your numbers are modest, real growth trajectory beats theoretical potential every time.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos who stuck with you through a major storm—that's gold, so show it. Add a line to your Rixos case study (Slide 4) stating something like "System operational 12+ months post-deployment with zero downtime" or "Customer renewed O&M contract for 3 years," which proves your product doesn't just work, it keeps working and customers want to keep paying for it. This turns a one-off win into evidence of sticky, profitable customer relationships.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project), EtihadWE

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market—right now you're comparing against product categories, not companies. Replace "Legacy Solar" with something like "Solahart systems" or "Apricus collectors," "Heat Pumps" with "Daikin or Midea units," and "Electric Storage" with "Tesla Powerwall or local battery providers"—this shows you've done competitive homework and makes your advantages concrete rather than theoretical.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a section called "Why Now, Why Us" that explains the specific barrier to entry—for example: "Flat-roof thermal systems require 18+ months of Gulf climate testing and certification; we've already completed this, giving us an 18-month head start before competitors can legally sell in GCC markets" or whatever your actual moat is (IP, supply chain, regulatory approval, customer lock-in, etc.).

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you can execute together—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and validate your strategy. For example, add a line like "Strategic Advisor: [Name], former VP Manufacturing at [relevant solar/thermal company], advising on supply chain scaling" or "Board Observer: [Name], led go-to-market for [comparable exit], guiding sales strategy"—this shows you're not just talented individuals but a coordinated team with outside validation.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" alongside his bio. This shows investors you've thought through retention incentives and prevents awkward questions later when they ask about it directly.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification mentioned on Slide 3, which is great, but investors in regulated markets want to see the full compliance picture upfront—add a dedicated compliance slide before your financials that maps out which UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel) your system meets, what certifications you already hold versus what you're pursuing by when, and how this de-risks adoption with developers and utilities. For example: "Solar Keymark ✓ | Dubai Municipality Approval (Q2 2026) | Estidama Gold Rating Path (Q3 2026) | Hard Water Warranty Compliance (UAE Standards 1065:2009)" —

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down across three jurisdictions and you're highlighting them well, but investors will want to know if anyone's challenging your IP or if there are any competing patent claims in the flat-roof solar space that could create friction down the line—add a one-sentence statement on Slide 8 (your IP moat slide) saying something like "No competing patent claims or ongoing disputes in the flat-roof solar thermal segment; freedom to operate confirmed" to give investors explicit confidence that your moat is clean and defensible.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing the math that connects your 5% UAE market share assumption to the $400M SAM figure—something like "5% of $400M SAM = $20M annual opportunity" so investors immediately see the relationship instead of having to reverse-engineer it themselves. This one sentence removes the ambiguity and shows disciplined thinking about your addressable market.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every [URL_REDACTED] placeholder and [UNKNOWN] label with actual content—Slide 3's title should be the product name (HelioFlow), and every URL should be a real link or removed entirely if it's not needed. This takes 30 minutes but signals to investors that your deck is final and investor-ready, not a work-in-progress template.
